title: Torn Dewclaw in Dogs: First Aid and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog bleeding from a torn dewclaw? Learn if it is an emergency, how to provide first aid, and why your vet needs a photo for triage.
content_html:
What is a Torn Dewclaw?

A dewclaw is the small, vestigial nail located higher up on your dog's leg. Because these nails do not touch the ground and wear down naturally, they can grow long and easily snag on carpet, brush, or furniture. When a dewclaw tears, it often exposes the 'quick,' which contains sensitive nerves and blood vessels, leading to pain and significant bleeding.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a torn dewclaw is typically Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening situation, it is very painful for your dog and carries a high risk of infection if left untreated. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours. However, if the bleeding is profuse and cannot be stopped with pressure, or if your dog is showing signs of extreme distress, seek urgent care.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your veterinarian, they may ask you to send a clear photo of the injury. A high-quality image allows the medical team to see exactly where the break occurred—whether the nail is still attached, dangling, or completely gone. This visual information helps them determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen and whether they should prepare for a simple trim or a more involved nail bed procedure.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: Apply firm, steady pressure with a clean cloth for 5 to 10 minutes. If you have styptic powder or cornstarch, you can dip the nail in it to help the blood clot.

faqs.question: Should I try to cut the dangling nail myself?
faqs.answer: It is generally best to wait for a professional. Cutting a damaged nail can be extremely painful and may cause your dog to bite out of reflex. A vet can provide local numbing or sedation if needed.

faqs.question: Will my dog need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Because the nail bed is close to the bone, vets often prescribe a short course of antibiotics and anti-inflammatory pain medication to prevent infection and manage discomfort.
